one_line_takeaway: A position paper arguing that securing LLM-enabled/agentic applications requires an EDR-style, workflow-stage-indexed audit-logging and monitoring layer -- complementary to (not a replacement for) red-teaming, guardrails, and alignment -- and it offers a 14-category threat taxonomy mapped to candidate monitoring artifacts as a starting schema, without any implementation or evaluation of that schema.
key_finding: There are no empirical findings, quantitative results, datasets, or baselines in this paper -- it contains zero experiments. Its substantive claims are argumentative: (a) pre-deployment testing, guardrails, and model alignment are each necessary but individually insufficient because they lack continuous, cross-stage, post-deployment visibility into an LLM application's runtime behavior; (b) a workflow-stage-indexed audit-logging schema (mapping each of the 14 threat categories' attack vectors to specific monitoring artifacts, e.g. Unicode-normalization diffs and tokens-per-character ratio for lexical-obfuscation attacks, or watermark-confidence-drop-per-training-step for fine-tuning-based watermark evasion) is proposed as the missing 'detection layer' that should feed an EDR-style incident-response lifecycle; (c) three specific open challenges are named for building such a framework: constructing and continually refreshing a corpus of suspicious patterns amid semantic ambiguity between legitimate complex instructions and malicious overrides; the added inference latency of LLM-based context inspection (for which the paper recommends a tiered pipeline of lightweight deterministic pre-filters plus selective LLM-based analysis only on flagged cases); and limited observability in closed-source/black-box deployments, which the paper argues motivates standardized, minimal-disclosure observability interfaces from cloud providers.
core_contribution: This is a position paper (ICML-style position-paper structure, including a mandatory 'Alternative Views' section) that argues LLM-enabled applications need dedicated, system-level, post-deployment threat monitoring modeled on traditional Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR), rather than relying solely on pre-deployment testing, guardrails, or model alignment. The paper's own constructive contribution is a taxonomy of 14 threat categories relevant to LLM-enabled/agentic applications operating under the Model Context Protocol (MCP), each decomposed into concrete attack vectors mapped onto an 8-stage generic agent workflow (user prompt -> MCP tool discovery -> brain reasoning -> external resource access -> tool planning -> tool execution -> final prompt assembly -> final response -> delivery). For each of the 14 categories the paper specifies candidate 'monitoring artifacts' (concrete signals a logging/audit pipeline could capture, e.g. Unicode-normalization diffs, embedding outlier scores, retrieval provenance metadata, watermark-confidence drop during fine-tuning) and sketches how an audit-logging pipeline would consume them, plus a short 'Post-monitoring Analysis' section describing an EDR-style triage/root-cause/containment lifecycle built on top of the monitoring layer.
threat_model: The paper does not present a single formal adversary model with capabilities/goals notation. Instead it enumerates 14 threat categories (with associated attack vectors) that it argues are intrinsic and unavoidable in deployed LLM-enabled applications operating via MCP-connected tools/RAG/memory: (1) Prompt Injection (direct, injected-in-RAG, via service API outputs), (2) Adversarial Inputs (lexical obfuscation, embedding-level attacks, adversarial multimodal/intermediate inputs), (3) Response Manipulation (prompt chaining/contextual drift, environment tampering and reuse, feedback gaming), (4) DoS or Unbounded Loops (oversized requests, recursive prompting, tool-call storms, adversarial cache-bypass), (5) Live Data Poisoning, (6) Live Model Poisoning (compromised update plane, runtime tampering, registry compromise), (7) Sensitive Data Leakage, (8) Cross-context Disclosure, (9) Memorisation Leakage, (10) Deployment Model Theft, (11) Watermark Removal and Fingerprint Evasion, (12) Model Drift, (13) Misinformation, (14) LLM-based Application Misuse. Scope is explicitly restricted to deployment-time threats intrinsic to the LLM component; training-stage attacks and generic (non-LLM) software vulnerabilities are declared out of scope. The taxonomy is stated to follow a forthcoming Singapore government technical reference on LLM application cybersecurity practices, i.e. it is largely adopted/organized by the authors rather than newly discovered.
limitations: The paper is explicitly a position/perspective piece with no prototype implementation, no evaluation of whether any proposed monitoring artifact actually detects the attacks it is paired with, and no quantification of false-positive/false-negative rates, latency overhead, or cost of the proposed tiered-inspection pipeline -- these tradeoffs are named as open challenges rather than resolved. The 14-category taxonomy is stated to follow a forthcoming (unpublished, as of this preprint) Singapore government technical reference, so its categorization scheme is largely inherited rather than independently validated by the authors. All attack examples throughout the paper are hypothetical illustrations constructed by the authors, not observed incidents or reproduced exploits. The paper itself acknowledges that black-box/closed-source deployments make its proposed audit-logging approach infeasible without provider cooperation, which it cannot resolve and instead frames as a call to action for cloud providers.
